coredata.dc:description:
Background Industrial dye pollution poses severe environmental threats, necessitating efficient and sustainable removal strategies. Additionally, forensic and biometric applications require high-resolution fingerprints (FPs) detection for accurate identification. This study develops carbon dots (CDs) integrated MoO3:1 %La3+ nanocomposites (NCs) for photocatalytic dye degradation and forensic applications, offering a multifunctional approach to environmental remediation and FP visualization.
Methods The NCs were synthesized using a green approach with neem leaf extracts and characterized via XRD, FTIR, SEM, TEM, XPS, and UV-Vis spectroscopy. Their photocatalytic efficiency was assessed through methyl orange (MO) degradation, while seed germination tests using Pisum sativum evaluated environmental safety. Additionally, the YOLOv8x deep-learning model was trained for enhanced latent fingerprint (LFP) detection and analysis.
Significant Findings NCs exhibited 99.4 % MO degradation within 70 min, detecting MO at concentrations as low as 0.032 μM. Post-treatment analysis confirmed complete degradation. Seed germination tests showed improved root (6.24 cm), shoot (7.83 cm), and germination energy (84 %), validating detoxification. NCs enhanced FP visualization, while YOLOv8x achieved over 90 % mean average precision (mAP) in minutiae detection, outperforming traditional methods. This work establishes CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs as a multifunctional solution for environmental remediation, agriculture, and forensic applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Industrial dye pollution poses severe environmental threats, necessitating efficient and sustainable removal strategies. Additionally, forensic and biometric applications require high-resolution fingerprints (FPs) detection for accurate identification. This study develops carbon dots (CDs) integrated MoO3:1 %La3+ nanocomposites (NCs) for photocatalytic dye degradation and forensic applications, offering a multifunctional approach to environmental remediation and FP visualization.

The NCs were synthesized using a green approach with neem leaf extracts and characterized via XRD, FTIR, SEM, TEM, XPS, and UV-Vis spectroscopy. Their photocatalytic efficiency was assessed through methyl orange (MO) degradation, while seed germination tests using Pisum sativum evaluated environmental safety. Additionally, the YOLOv8x deep-learning model was trained for enhanced latent fingerprint (LFP) detection and analysis.

NCs exhibited 99.4 % MO degradation within 70 min, detecting MO at concentrations as low as 0.032 μM. Post-treatment analysis confirmed complete degradation. Seed germination tests showed improved root (6.24 cm), shoot (7.83 cm), and germination energy (84 %), validating detoxification. NCs enhanced FP visualization, while YOLOv8x achieved over 90 % mean average precision (mAP) in minutiae detection, outperforming traditional methods. This work establishes CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs as a multifunctional solution for environmental remediation, agriculture, and forensic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution continues to be a pressing global issue, contributing to an estimated 14,000 deaths daily due to pollution-related causes. Both developed and developing nations grapple with deteriorating water quality caused by diverse factors such as climate, soil composition, vegetation, and anthropogenic activities. Among the various pollutants, organic contaminants, especially dyes, are particularly concerning due to their persistence and inadequate discharge management, further exacerbating water contamination. Effective solutions demand continuous policy reassessment and innovative approaches [1–6].

The textile industry, accounting for over 6 % of global exports and employing approximately 30 million individuals, is a major contributor to dye pollution. Even at concentrations as low as 1 ppm, these dyes pose significant health risks, entering the human body through ingestion and causing harm to both humans and animals. Resistant to conventional treatments, these dyes adversely impact ecosystems and public health. Studies have documented their toxicological effects, including carcinogenic, mutagenic, and teratogenic impacts, with by-products compounding the threat [7–9]. For instance, research by Islam et al. [10] revealed that exposure to textile dye wastewater caused reduced hematocrit levels, hemoglobin, and sperm concentration, as well as structural changes in the testes of Swiss albino mice. Additional studies reported severe liver and intestinal damage in animals exposed to untreated textile dyes, underscoring their widespread toxicity [11].

MO, an anionic azo dye, is extensively used in textiles, paper, food, leather industries, and as a pH indicator in research [12–15]. Despite its utility, MO poses a serious threat to ecological balance and human health due to its high mutagenic and carcinogenic potential. Upon accidental ingestion, intestinal and liver reductase enzymes degrade MO's azo group into aromatic amines, compounds linked to intestinal cancers [16]. MO also disrupts biological wastewater treatment processes, emphasizing the need for advanced strategies to degrade this hazardous dye. Photocatalysis has emerged as a promising approach for dye degradation, with titanium dioxide (TiO2) being a widely studied heterogeneous photocatalyst. However, its performance is limited to the UV spectrum due to its 3.2 eV band gap, low solar absorption efficiency, and practical challenges like aggregation, poor adsorption of hydrophobic pollutants, and recovery difficulties [17]. To address these limitations, researchers have explored molybdenum trioxide (MoO3), a transition metal oxide with a 3.15 eV band gap and a high work function of 5.4 eV. Known for its catalytic properties in various applications, including battery electrodes, imaging systems, and gas sensors, MoO3 offers superior adsorption behavior compared to other oxide semiconductors [18]. However, its photocatalytic activity can be enhanced through strategies like doping with metals or forming nanocomposites (NCs) with materials like graphene and carbon nitride to increase active sites and thermal stability [19–23]. Table 1 [24–32] summarizes recent advancements in green synthesis methods for MoO3 nanoparticles, highlighting the role of plant extracts in reducing metal sources to NPs.

Lanthanide ion-doped nanomaterials often suffer from reduced luminescence quantum efficiency due to surface imperfections, low molar absorptivity, narrow absorption bands, and other factors. As a result, improving surface defects and enhancing luminescence quantum efficiency is essential to overcome these limitations. CDs serve as sensitizers and surface modifiers, enhancing photoluminescence (PL) by tuning the optical band gap of target materials. Their exceptional properties, including low toxicity, high stability, and strong luminescence, have made them increasingly popular, driving their application across various fields. Incorporating CDs into phosphors has enhanced their morphology and luminous efficiency [33]. Recent studies on CD-RE-doped luminescent composites, such as CDs@Y2TiWO8:Ce3+ and CDs@ Ca2SiO4:Ce3+, have demonstrated that Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) from CDs to Ce3+ significantly boosts photoluminescence intensity [34,35]. Similarly, CDs@ GdAlO3:Eu3+/Li+ exhibited higher PL intensity than GdAlO3:Eu3+ due to the FRET mechanism between CDs and Eu3+ [36].

Fingerprint (FP) analysis has been regarded as a highly reliable method for personal identification since the 19th century, owing to the unique and permanent nature of fingerprints [37]. Although most fingerprints are not visible to the naked eye, residues left by finger secretions form distinct ridge patterns on surfaces, commonly referred to as LFPs [38]. Consequently, researchers have explored various techniques for detecting and identifying LFPs, including fluorescent dye staining, powder dusting, vacuum metal deposition, and cyanoacrylate/iodine fuming [39]. However, these methods often suffer from limitations such as low contrast, poor sensitivity, low selectivity, and high toxicity, which restrict their widespread use in LFP imaging [40]. Therefore, there is a need to develop novel materials with low toxicity and excellent stability for more effective LFP imaging. Additionally, most previous studies have primarily focused on LFP imaging while overlooking their analysis and comparison. With the rapid advancement of biometric and criminal investigation technology, developing an integrated and rapid identification method is crucial, as traditional manual analysis and identification remain inefficient and subjective.

Recognition systems incorporating the YOLOv8x model have gained attention for their speed and accuracy [41]. YOLOv8x's advanced deep learning architecture enhances real-time fingerprint detection and feature extraction with superior precision. Unlike traditional minutiae-based methods, it employs a transformer-based backbone and optimized convolutional layers to detect and classify fingerprints, even in noisy or partial prints. Its low-latency processing enables real-time biometric applications, such as security authentication and forensic analysis [42]. Additionally, its adaptive anchor-free detection and dynamic optimization improve robustness against distortions and environmental variations. To date, no successful integration of LFP detection with an identification system has been reported. Therefore, developing novel materials and establishing an integrated analytical system for imaging and comparing LFPs remain critical challenges for advancing LFP applications.

Herein, we explored novel, highly stable, efficient La3+ doped MoO3 NPs. As far as we know, no study has been done on their nanocomposites with CDs. The unique CDs@MoO3:La3+ NCs was fabricated using green combustion method. The prepared NCs can be used for photocatalytic dye degradation, LFP detection coupled with YOLOv8x deep learning model. These findings establish a sustainable and multifunctional approach for environmental remediation and forensic science advancements. These findings establish a sustainable and multifunctional approach for environmental remediation and forensic science advancements.

Fresh Azadirachta indica (neem) leaves were collected from the gardens of Tumkur University, Tumkur, India. The chemicals used in this study included ammonium heptamolybdate [(NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O], lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate [La(NO3)3·6H2O], malachite green, methylene blue, rhodamine 6G, and methyl orange, all procured from Sigma-Aldrich with a purity of 99.9 %. A 1 mM stock solution of each dye was prepared in Milli-Q water and stored in the dark for further use. Additionally, 1,4-benzoquinone, EDTA, and isopropyl alcohol, also of 99.9 % purity, were sourced from Sigma-Aldrich. Interfering substances such as mannose, glucose, sucrose, chitosan, ascorbic acid, dopamine, L-cysteine, and uric acid, with 99 % purity, were purchased from the same supplier. Chloride salts of various cations, including Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Ni+, Na+, Pb2+ and Zn2+ with ≥99 % purity, were also obtained from Sigma-Aldrich.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs using Azadirachta Indica leaves
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of CDs followed a green synthesis route utilizing neem leaves. Fresh Azadirachta indica leaves were thoroughly washed with ethanol and double-distilled water to remove surface contaminants. After cleaning, the leaves were oven-dried at 150°C for 4 h (Fig. 1 a). The dried leaves were then ground into fine powder using a mortar and pestle. This powder was calcined in a muffle furnace at 300°C for 4 h and subsequently allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. To extract CDs, 2 g of the calcined powder was dissolved in 500 mL of distilled water and stirred overnight at 4000 rpm. The resultant suspension was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm to remove larger aggregates. The solution was further purified by dialysis against pure water using a dialysis membrane for 24 h to eliminate small impurities. Finally, the purified solution was dried in a silica crucible at 100°C, yielding fine and pure CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Azadirachta Indica leaf extract
ce:section.ce:para: Azadirachta indica leaves from the uppermost branches were handpicked from the Tumkur University campus and washed thoroughly with double-distilled water to remove any impurities. The cleaned leaves were cut into small pieces and processed in a borosilicate conical flask containing 100 mL of distilled water and 10 g of fresh leaves. The mixture was boiled for 30 min, after which it was filtered through Whatman No.1 filter paper. The resultant extract, shown schematically in Fig. 1b, was stored for further use in the synthesis of NCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of La-doped MoO3 using neem leaf extract
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs involved a green approach utilizing Azadirachta indica extract. Stoichiometric amounts of ammonium heptamolybdate and lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate were dissolved in approximately 100 mL of double-distilled water under magnetic stirring at 100°C for 30 min. Subsequently, 10 mL of neem leaf extract was added dropwise to the mixture. The solution was then dried at an appropriate temperature, ground into powder, and calcined at 550°C for 3 h. A schematic representation of the synthesis process is provided in Fig. 1b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs with CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The functionalization of MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs with CDs was performed to enhance their properties. Specifically, 5.0 mg of synthesized MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs was dissolved in 1 mL of deionized water and introduced into a 25 mL reaction flask containing 10 mL of a CDs solution. The mixture was subjected to ultrasonication for 1 h to ensure uniform dispersion and functionalization, followed by allowing the reaction to proceed at room temperature. After completion, the resultant gel was dried in a vacuum chamber at 100°C for 2 h. A schematic representation of the functionalization process is shown in Fig. 1c. The final composite material was stored for further characterization and experimentation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference response of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs toward MO dye
ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescence-based experiments were conducted to assess the selectivity and sensitivity of the synthesized CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs derived from Azadirachta indica leaves. The photoluminescence spectra were recorded after adding 1 mL of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs to analytes such as metal ions, sugars, and dyes, including Li+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Cr2+, K+, Zn2+, Na+, Mannose, Sucrose, Glucose, Cysteine, Methylene Blue, Rhodamine 6G, Methyl orange and Malachite Green. The mixtures were incubated for 2 min before fluorescence studies. Interference studies were performed to determine the ability of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs to detect MO in the presence of other analytes. Experiments were conducted in triplicate, and the average results were plotted, demonstrating the selectivity and interference tolerance of the NCs.

All spectroscopic experiments were conducted at neutral pH using Milli-Q water. A standard stock solution of 1 mM MO was prepared in a 50 mL volumetric flask. Various concentrations of MO were prepared, and an aliquot of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs was added to each sample. The mixtures were vigorously shaken and incubated for 2 min before measuring absorbance and fluorescence intensity. Spectrofluorometric measurements were performed using an excitation wavelength of 280 nm with a slit width of 10 nm. Sensitivity studies, including the calculation of the limit of detection (LOD), were also carried out. Furthermore, interference studies were conducted to investigate the influence of other chemicals on the detection of MO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation response of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs toward Mo
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic properties of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs synthesized from Azadirachta indica leaves were evaluated through the degradation of methyl orange. Photocatalytic experiments were performed in a reactor equipped with a stirrer and a sun light. The light source was positioned 15 cm from the reactor. For the photocatalytic setup, 20 mg of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs was added to a 20 μM solution of MO dye and stirred magnetically for 10 min in the dark to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The solution was then irradiated under sun light. A control experiment was performed in the absence of light to evaluate adsorption effects alone. At regular intervals, 2 mL aliquots were withdrawn, centrifuged, and analyzed for degradation efficiency by monitoring changes in the absorbance spectrum. The degradation efficiency (%) was calculated using the formula [43]:

where Co represents initial concentration of dye and C stands for concentration at regular time interval.

To ensure sterility and prevent bacterial contamination, all selected seeds were initially sterilized in a mercuric chloride (HgCl2) solution for 5 min. Following sterilization, the seeds were thoroughly rinsed with deionized water for 10–15 min to remove any residual HgCl2. The effect of photo catalytically treated water on the germination of pea (Pisum sativum) seeds was assessed. In this experiment, seeds pre-soaked in the treated water samples were placed on germination sheets inside Petri plates, with 10 healthy seeds per plate. The plates were incubated under dark conditions at 28 ± 3°C for 72 h. The time required for seed germination and the number of seeds germinated in each plate were recorded. At the end of the incubation period, various growth parameters were measured, including germination rate, mean germination time, mean shoot length (cm), mean root length (cm), fresh weight (mg), and dry weight (mg) of the seedlings. Each treatment was performed in quintuplicate, and the mean ± standard deviation (SD) was calculated for all results. Several germination parameters were calculated using the following formulas [44]:

Here, the total number of germinated seeds for degraded water and control is represented by St and Sc, respectively. The length of the germinated seedlings degraded water and control is shown by Lt and Lc. The average root length of the seeds in the degraded water and control is denoted by Cd and Cc, respectively.

LFPs were obtained from a 25-year-old female donor on various surfaces, including porous surfaces (wood), semi-porous surfaces (compact disc), and non-porous surfaces (OHP sheets). Before producing the FPs, the donor washed their hands with soap and water and gently rubbed their fingers against the nose or forehead to create consistent oil deposits. The LFPs were developed using the synthesized CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs, which were dusted onto the surfaces with a soft feather brush. Excess powder was removed until the ridge features became clearly visible. The developed FPs were photographed using a Canon DSLR camera equipped with a 90 mm F/2.8 macro lens under natural sunlight. Additionally, a Python-based program was utilized for rapid enhancement and minutiae extraction to identify FP features effectively.

XRD patterns were recorded using a Shimadzu-7000 X-ray diffractometer with monochromatic CuKα radiation (2θ = 10–70 °). Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) was performed using a JEOL JSM 7500F instrument, coupled with energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy for elemental analysis. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was conducted on a Hitachi H-7500 microscope operating at 100 kV to study particle morphology and size. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was carried out using a PerkinElmer RXI spectrometer within the range of 400–4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected using an ESCALAB 250xi spectrometer to analyze surface composition and oxidation states. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a LABINDIA UV–vis spectrophotometer within the 200–800 nm range. Quartz cuvettes with a 1 cm path length were used for all measurements.

The XRD patterns of MoO3, MoO3:1 %La3+, CDs, and CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs are presented in Fig. 2 a. The distinct diffraction peaks observed for MoO3 and MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs correspond to the crystal planes as indexed in Fig. 2a. These results align with the orthorhombic crystal structure in the α-phase, confirmed by JCPDS card no. 05-508 [45]. The sharp and well-defined diffraction peaks suggest excellent crystallinity of the α -MoO3 phase, with no additional phases detected, indicating high purity of the samples. Lanthanum ion doping did not introduce any detectable secondary phases, such as La2O3​, likely due to the low doping concentration. This confirms that the substitutional incorporation of La ions did not disrupt the orthorhombic structure of MoO3. The characteristic (020) peak at 2θ=12.60° is evident across all samples, verifying the retention of the orthorhombic phase post-doping [46]. For the CDs, the broad peak at 2θ=20° corresponds to the (002) plane, indicative of amorphous carbon. The XRD pattern of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs includes both the characteristic peaks of MoO3:1 %La3+ and the broad peak of CDs, confirming the successful incorporation of CDs into the MoO3:1 %La3+ matrix [47].

Rietveld refinement was performed to confirm the orthorhombic phase stability following La doping are represented in Fig. 2b and 2c. The lattice parameter and unit cell volume are obtained from the output file of Rietveld refinement. The lattice parameters increase for MoO3:1 %La3+. Unit cell volume also displays similar increment as lattice parameters. The raw XRD data has been processed using Fullprof Suite software coupled with WinPLOTR [48]. In the Rietveld method, the Pseudo-Voigt function, a linear combination of Gaussian and Lorentzian functions, was employed to fit the peak profile accurately. Fig. 2b and 2d displays the experimental data, Yobs, the calculated data, Ycal, and the difference between observed and calculated data, Yobs − Ycal and The vertical line represents the permissible Bragg's 2θ location. The values of certain parameters, obtained through iterative refinement cycles that guarantee refinement quality, are reported in Table S1. These parameters include Rp (profile fitting R-value), Rwp (weighted profile), χ2 (quality factor for goodness-of-fit), Rexp (R-value of the expected pattern), lattice constants, and cell volume. GOF, expressed as the S = Rwp/Rexp, indicates the goodness of fit, where Rwp is the R-value of the weighted profile, and the Rexp is the R-value of the expected pattern. All the parameters were obtained after repeated iterations till convergent. Orthorhombic α-MoO3 is a layered oxide with zigzag chains of MoO6 octahedra interconnected by shared vertices and edges. The layers interact via van der Waals forces along [010], maintaining a lamellar structure. The MoO6 octahedra are distorted, with varying Mo-O bond lengths, where the strongest apical Mo=O bond lies along [010], acting as a terminal double bond. Within layers, Mo-O-Mo linkages form along [100] and [001], with oxygen bridging octahedra differently in each direction. In [100], oxygen links two octahedra with distinct bond strengths, while in [001], bridging oxygens connect octahedra within the same and adjacent layers [49]. The Fig. 2d highlights this zigzag octahedral connectivity and the crucial role of oxygen in structural integrity.

The crystallite size and lattice strain of the synthesized NPs were estimated using the Size-Strain Plot (SSP) method. This approach assumes isotropic crystallite shapes, where crystallite size is represented by Lorentzian broadening and strain by Gaussian broadening. The equation used is [50]:
(6)
(
d β C o s θ
)

k D

d
2
β C o s θ
)

(
є 2
)
2

A linear graph of (dβCosθ)2 versus d2βCosθ was plotted, and the slope and intercept provided values for crystallite size (D) and strain (ε). The results are summarized in Table S2, and the corresponding SSP plots are shown in Fig. S1.

The morphological analysis of the synthesized samples was conducted using FESEM, as shown in Fig. 3a–3d . The micrograph of MoO3 (Fig. 3a) reveals significant agglomeration and irregular particle sizes. Upon La doping, the morphology of MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs (Fig. 3b) was modified, showing improved particle uniformity. The FESEM image of CDs (Fig. 3c) illustrates a uniform distribution of small particles in close proximity. For CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs, the material exhibits a mix of nanostructures, including spheres, cones, and rods, as shown in Fig. 3d. This morphological diversity is attributed to the use of the extract and La doping during synthesis. These nanostructures provide a high surface-to-volume ratio and abundant active sites, enhancing the photocatalytic efficiency of the composite [51]. Fig. 3e shows the magnified SEM image of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs, highlights the successful integration of CDs as distinct, smaller particles adhered to the MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs. This integration is expected to improve optical properties due to synergistic interactions between CDs and MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs, making the composite highly effective for luminescence and photocatalytic applications.

Fig. 3f displays the TEM image of MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs, revealing sheet-like structures with non-uniform sizes and minimal agglomeration. The observed particle sizes range between 30 and 60 nm. As shown in Fig. 3g, TEM analysis was conducted to examine the microscopic features of CDs. The results reveal that the particles are spherical, uniformly monodispersed, and exhibit minimal aggregation [52]. Fig. 3h shows the particle size distribution histogram of CDs and the average particle size is estimated to be 7.12±1.97 nm. The TEM image of the CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs is shown in Fig. 3i & j. The integration of CDs into the phosphor is clearly visible, with the size of the CDs ranging from 4 to 8 nm, while the phosphor particles are observed to be between 30 and 60 nm. Additionally, particle size distribution histograms for the CDs in the composite are presented in Fig. 3k and the average particle size of CDs in NCs is found to be 7.05±1.89 nm.

The HR-TEM images of MoO3:1La3+, CDs, and CDs/MoO3:1La3+ NCs are presented in Fig. 4 a, c, and e. The well-defined lattice fringes observed in these images indicate high crystallinity. An interplanar distance (d) of 0.29 nm, corresponding to the (130) plane of MoO3:1La3+, is identified in Fig. 4e. Additionally, the d-spacing of the (002) plane of CDs is estimated to be 0.32 nm. In the CDs/MoO3:1La3+ NCs, the estimated d-values for the NPs and CDs are 0.29 nm (130) and 0.33 nm (002), respectively. Furthermore, line profile diagrams generated using GATAN software confirm these d-spacing values, aligning well with the HR-TEM results (Fig. 4h-k). The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs (Fig. 4l) exhibits a well-ordered diffraction lattice with a uniform array of diffraction spots, recorded perpendicular to the growth axis. These patterns confirm the single-crystal nature of the α-MoO3 NPs [53,54]. The observed diffraction planes correspond to the orthorhombic α-MoO3 phase, with Miller indices of (021), (040), and (110). These results align with the XRD analysis, further validating the high crystallinity of the synthesized material.

Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy was utilized to determine the elemental composition of the synthesized materials. The EDX spectrum of MoO3 NPs (Fig. S2a) confirms the presence of molybdenum (Mo) and oxygen (O), with respective proportions of 32.4 % and 67.6 %, as shown in Fig. S2b. For MoO3:1 %La3+, the addition of lanthanum is evident, as La peaks appear in the spectrum (Fig. S2c). The introduction of La (7.1 %) reduces the Mo content, as observed in Fig. S2d. The EDX spectrum of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs (Fig. S2e) reveals an additional peak for carbon, along with peaks for Mo, O, and La, confirming the successful incorporation of CDs. When CDs are added to MoO3:1 %La3+, a small carbon content (0.3 %) is detected, as shown in Fig. S2f. The dominance of Mo and O peaks across all samples indicates the integrity of the MoO3 matrix, while the absence of extraneous peaks confirms the high purity of the synthesized materials [55].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to investigate the elemental composition and chemical states of the surface in the synthesized materials. The survey spectra of MoO3:1 %La3+ and CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs are shown in Fig. 5 a. Both spectra exhibit similar profiles, with an increased intensity observed for the C 1s peak at ∼286.6 eV in the CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs, indicating the presence of CDs in the NC. The Mo 3d XPS spectra of MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs and CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs (Fig. 5b) feature two prominent peaks at 233.1 eV and 236.2 eV, corresponding to the Mo 3d₅/2 and Mo 3d3/2 orbitals, respectively. These binding energies are characteristic of Mo⁶⁺ in the MoO3 structure. Importantly, the binding energies of Mo⁶⁺ ions remain consistent in both MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs and CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs, confirming the retention of the MoO3 framework upon CDs incorporation. The O 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 5c) for both MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs and CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs display three distinct peaks at 530.9 eV, 531.7 eV, and 534.7 eV, corresponding to lattice oxygen (Oa), oxygen vacancies (Ob), and chemisorbed oxygen (Oc), respectively. The significant increase in the area ratio of oxygen vacancies (Ob) in CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs compared to MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs is attributed to the incorporation of CDs. These oxygen vacancies play a critical role in enhancing the catalytic activity by promoting target oxidation reactions on the material's surface. The La 3d XPS spectra of MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs and CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs (Fig. 5d) exhibit peaks at binding energies of 833.8 eV and 838.6 eV for the La 3d₅/2 orbital and 853.7 eV and 864.1 eV for the La 3d3/2 orbital, confirming the successful doping of La ions into the MoO3 structure [56,57]. The C 1s XPS spectrum of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs (Fig. 5e) reveals peaks at 285.1 eV, 286.6 eV, and 288.8 eV, corresponding to C–C, C–O, and O=C–N bonds, respectively, which signify the presence of functional groups in the CDs. The N 1s XPS spectrum of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs (Fig. 5f) displays three peaks at 399.2 eV, 400.7 eV, and 403.1 eV, corresponding to C=N–C (pyridinic N), C=N (pyrrolic N), and C–N–H (graphitic N) bonds, respectively [58]. These functional groups contribute to the enhanced chemical reactivity and stability of the NC.

Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of MoO3, MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs, CDs, and CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs are shown in Fig. S3a. The prominent peak at 460 cm⁻¹ corresponds to the stretching vibrations of Mo–O–Mo bonds, confirming the formation of the MoO3 framework [59]. Additional peaks at 958, 878, and 630 cm⁻¹ are associated with C–H, HC=O, and C–O stretching vibrations, respectively, indicating interactions between phytochemical constituents and the synthesized materials, which may influence their physicochemical properties during catalytic processes [60]. The FTIR spectrum of CDs (Fig. S3a) exhibits characteristic peaks in the range of 3454–3344 cm⁻¹, corresponding to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations. These functional groups confer hydrophilic properties and stability to the CDs. A minor peak at 2973 cm⁻¹ corresponds to C–H bond stretching, while peaks at 1689 cm⁻¹ and 1598 cm⁻¹ are attributed to O=C–O and C=C bond stretching, respectively. Additional peaks at 1208 cm⁻¹ and 1030 cm⁻¹ correspond to C–H/C–N and N–H stretching vibrations, respectively. A peak near 851 cm⁻¹ is linked to stretching vibrations of C–O, CH2, and C–N bonds [61]. The presence of identical bands in the FTIR spectra of both CDs and MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs in the NCs confirms the successful incorporation of CDs into the MoO3:1 %La3+ matrix. Additionally, the observed hydroxyl (–OH) groups play a crucial role in enhancing photocatalytic activity by facilitating various catalytic mechanisms [62].

UV–vis spectroscopy was employed to evaluate the absorbance behavior of the synthesized materials. The spectra of MoO3, MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs, and CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs are shown in Fig. S3b. The CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs exhibited significantly extended absorbance in the 400–700 nm range of the visible spectrum compared to MoO3 and MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs. This enhanced absorbance strength is attributed to the intercalation of La and the incorporation of CDs, which increase the charge carrier concentration, resulting in a plasmonic absorption effect [63]. This broad absorption in the visible region demonstrates that the material is an effective visible-light-activated photocatalyst. The incorporation of CDs and doping of La in the MoO3 matrix introduce new energy levels between the valence and conduction bands, facilitating enhanced visible light absorption. The energy band gaps (Eg​) of MoO3, MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs, and CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs were estimated using Tauc plots (Fig. S3c). The calculated Eg​ values are 3.17 eV, 3.02 eV, and 2.82 eV, respectively. The reduction in the band gap of MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs is due to La doping, while the further reduction in the CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs is attributed to the incorporation of CDs, as La doping remained constant across both samples [64]. The reduced band gap of 2.82 eV for CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs highlights their suitability for visible-light-driven photocatalytic applications.

The photocatalytic efficiency of MoO3, MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs, and CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs was evaluated by analysing the degradation of MO dye under visible light. Factors such as surface area, light absorption, crystal structure, defects, and charge carrier separation contribute to the photocatalytic performance of a material. The results of photocatalytic experiments are presented in Fig. S4a and S4b, showing the degradation performance of the materials after 20 and 80 min of light exposure, respectively. The CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs exhibited superior photocatalytic activity compared to MoO3 and MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs. The degradation efficiency of MO dye after 80 min was 99.2 % for CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs, significantly higher than 32.6 % and 48.8 % for MoO3 and MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs, respectively, as shown in Fig. S4d. The higher efficiency of MoO3:1 %La3+ compared to MoO3 is attributed to the reduced crystal size, which increases the active surface area and the number of active sites required for pollutant degradation. Additionally, La doping introduces new energy levels, disrupting charge recombination (electron-hole pairs) and enhancing the formation of reactive hydroxyl species (OH∙) responsible for dye degradation [65].

The incorporation of CDs into MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs further boosts photocatalytic performance by extending light absorption into the visible spectrum and improving charge carrier separation. CDs act as electron acceptors and donors, reducing recombination rates and facilitating efficient charge transfer. Moreover, CDs introduce new energy levels within the band gap and may induce plasmonic effects, further enhancing photocatalytic activity [66]. As shown in Fig. S4c, the concentration of MO dye decreased steadily over time, indicating continuous degradation. The reaction kinetics of the catalysts was represented following Langmuir-Hinshelhood equation (Eq. (7)) [67].
(7)
k t = − ln
(
C t
C 0
)

Here, k is the apparent reaction rate constant, t is the irradiation time, Ct is the concentration of the MO dye at time (t), and C0 is its initial concentration. As can be seen in the Fig. 6 a, the photocatalytic degradation followed a pseudo-first-order kinetic model [68]. The calculated rate constants (k) for the different photocatalysts are provided in Fig. 6a. Notably, the CDs/MoO3:1%La3+ NCs exhibited the highest k value of 0.044 min-1, which is 13.93 times higher than that of pure MoO3 (0.0032 min-1) (Fig. 6b). This enhanced rate constant underscores the superior photocatalytic performance of CDs/MoO3:1%La3+ NCs. Based on these results, CDs/MoO3:1%La3+ NCs was implying the high degradation performance. Compared to previously reported photocatalysts listed in Table 2 , the synthesized CDs/MoO3:1%La3+ NCs demonstrate superior photocatalytic degradation efficiency [69–78]. Due to their high degradation efficiency and robust performance under visible light, CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs are identified as a highly effective photocatalyst. This makes them promising candidates for advanced applications in environmental remediation and pollutant degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective sensing response of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs towards Mo dye
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the selective sensing capabilities of Azadirachta indica-derived CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs, their optical properties were analyzed through UV–vis and Fluorescence studies in the presence of various analytes. These analytes included metal ions, sugars, and dyes such as Li+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Cr2+, K+, Zn2+, Na+, mannose, sucrose, glucose, cysteine, methylene blue, rhodamine 6G, MO, and malachite green (MG). As shown in Fig. 7 a, the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs was significantly quenched in the presence of MO dye compared to other analytes, indicating high selectivity toward MO. The fluorescence quenching behaviour was further investigated as a function of MO dye concentration at an emission wavelength (λemi ​) of 437 nm, excited at 300 nm (Fig. 7b). A 35 % decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed as the MO concentration increased from 0 to 50 μM, demonstrating the sensitivity of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs toward MO. To assess the interference of other analytes on the sensing response, additional experiments were conducted (Fig. 7c). A normalized plot showed that the fluorescence response of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs toward MO remained unaffected in the presence of other species, confirming its high selectivity and potential for sensing MO in wastewater samples (Chauhan et al., 2022). The fluorescence quenching of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs in the presence of MO dye was analyzed using the Stern–Volmer (SV) equation [79]:
(8)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
Q

Here, Fo and F represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs in the absence and presence of MO, respectively. Ksv ​ is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, and [Q] is the concentration of MO dye. The SV plot (Fig. 7d) displayed a strong linear relationship with a correlation coefficient of 0.9838, indicating good predictability of the fluorescence quenching mechanism within the tested concentration range. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using the equation [80]:
(9)
L O D =
3
× S . D
P
where S.D. is the standard deviation, and P is the slope of the Stern–Volmer plot. The LOD for MO was determined to be 0.032 μM, indicating the NCs ability to detect very low concentrations of the analyte. The limit of quantitation (LOQ), ensuring reliable and accurate measurements, was found to be 0.103 μM. The results confirm that CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs are highly selective, sensitive, and reliable for detecting MO dye, making them suitable for environmental monitoring and wastewater treatment applications [81].

The proposed photocatalytic mechanism for MO dye degradation using CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs is schematically illustrated in Fig. 8 a. Initially, MO dye molecules are adsorbed onto the surface of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs, where the photocatalytic reaction begins. Upon exposure to visible light, the MO dye molecules are excited to higher energy states (MO*). These excited electrons are transferred from MO* to the conduction band (CB) of MoO3:1 %La3+, generating reactive species such as MO⁺•, essential for subsequent photocatalytic reactions. The energy level of MO* lies slightly above the CB of MoO3:1 %La3+, providing the driving force for electron injection. Holes in the valence band (VB) of MoO3:1 %La3+ exhibit strong oxidative potential, while electrons in the CB possess reductive capabilities. CDs act as electron reservoirs, facilitating efficient electron transfer and reducing electron-hole recombination rates. Upon irradiation, electrons in the CDs are transferred to the CB of MoO3:1 %La3+, resulting in enhanced separation of electron-MO⁺• pairs and improved photocatalytic efficiency. In the solution, the transferred electrons interact with oxygen to form reactive oxygen species (O2−​), which play a crucial role in the photodegradation process. The sensitization effect of CDs significantly enhances visible light photocatalytic activity. Additionally, the shift in the absorption peak and decrease in absorbance can be attributed to the demethylation of MO dye molecules, a process involving the removal of a methyl group (CH3) [82]. The degradation of MO dye is visually evident as the solution transitions from an intense orange color to almost colorless, as shown in Fig. 8a. This color change confirms the substantial degradation of the dye facilitated by CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs [83].

The photocatalytic performance of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs was assessed by evaluating the degradation of MO dye under visible light. Fig. 8b demonstrates the initial absorbance spectrum in the absence of a catalyst. The negligible change in absorbance during blank tests confirms that dye degradation is primarily driven by the presence of the photocatalyst. Fig. 8c illustrates the photocatalytic degradation behavior of MO dye as a function of time. The efficiency of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs depends on structural, morphological, and size-related parameters. The reduction in absorbance intensity indicates the adsorption of dye molecules and their subsequent degradation, confirming the catalytic role of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs. As depicted in Fig. 8d, the concentration of MO dye decreases steadily, achieving near-complete removal within 70 min. Fig. 8e presents the degradation efficiency as a function of irradiation time, with varying concentrations of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs. The results demonstrate that a concentration of 0.4 mg/mL of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs can degrade MO dye (40 mg/L) by 99.04 % within 70 min of exposure. Additionally, the photocatalytic behavior of the catalyst was further explored under varying dye concentrations and pH conditions. The findings indicate that CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs exhibit robust photocatalytic activity, making them a highly effective material for the degradation of organic pollutants under visible light.

The pH of the solution plays a critical role in determining the photocatalytic degradation rate, as it directly affects the interaction between the photocatalyst and the dye molecules. This study investigated the degradation efficiency of MO dye across pH levels ranging from 3 to 10 using 0.4 mg/mL of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs and 40 mg/L of MO under 70 min of irradiation (Fig. S5a–c). The results reveal that the highest degradation efficiency (99 %) occurred at pH 6, which aligns with the zero-point charge (pHzpc​) of MoO3 (4.3) as reported by S. Srinivasan et al. [84]. At pH values close to pHzpc​, the surface charge of MoO3 becomes positively charged, enhancing its electrostatic attraction to negatively charged MO dye molecules. This protonation of the catalyst surface facilitates dye molecule adsorption and subsequent degradation. These findings emphasize the importance of pH in optimizing the photocatalytic performance of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs. The initial concentration of dye is another significant factor influencing the photocatalytic degradation process, as it determines the availability of dye molecules to interact with the photocatalyst. Experiments were conducted with MO dye concentrations ranging from 10 to 80 mg/L to assess the maximum potential of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs (Fig. S5d–f).

The results demonstrate a slight decrease in degradation efficiency with increasing dye concentration. This reduction can be attributed to the obstruction of the photon pathway by the higher density of dye molecules, which limits photon access to the photocatalyst surface and slows the degradation rate [85]. Nevertheless, the photocatalyst achieved a degradation efficiency of 98.6 % at the highest dye concentration (80 mg/L), indicating its robustness and efficiency even under high dye loads. These findings confirm that CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs maintain exceptional photocatalytic activity across varying dye concentrations. The influence of photocatalyst dosage on the degradation efficiency of MO dye (40 mg/L) was explored using CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs at concentrations ranging from 0.2 mg/mL to 1.6 mg/mL. The degradation behavior was monitored through UV–vis absorption spectroscopy (Fig. S5g–i). The results indicate that degradation efficiency increases with higher photocatalyst concentrations. This improvement is due to the increased availability of surface-active sites, which enhances the interaction between the photocatalyst and dye molecules, accelerating the photocatalytic reaction. The data demonstrate that optimizing the photocatalyst dosage is crucial for achieving faster degradation rates and higher efficiencies in photocatalytic applications.

The economic feasibility of photocatalytic degradation processes is significantly influenced by the reusability of the chosen photocatalyst. To assess the recyclability of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs for MO dye degradation, repeated degradation tests were conducted. After each cycle, the photocatalyst was recovered via centrifugation, washed with ethanol and water to remove any residual impurities, and dried before being reused in a fresh MO dye solution under light irradiation. The degradation efficiency and the C/C0​ curve for each cycle are presented in Fig. S6a and S6b, respectively. The results demonstrate remarkable stability, with only a slight decrease of ∼1 % in degradation efficiency after five cycles. This consistent performance indicates that CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs retain their catalytic activity and structural integrity over multiple uses. XRD analysis of the CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs after five cycles (Fig. S6c) showed no significant changes in structural phases, confirming the robustness of the catalyst. A minor shift in peak intensity was observed, likely due to the removal of dye-derived impurities. Similarly, the FTIR spectrum (Fig. S6d) revealed a stronger and broader peak at 1036 cm⁻¹, attributed to C–O stretching vibrations, indicating the formation of organic byproducts from degraded dye solutions [86]. These findings confirm that CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs exhibit excellent stability and recyclability, maintaining their structural and chemical properties even after multiple photocatalytic cycles. This robustness underscores their potential for efficient and repeated use in wastewater treatment applications.

To evaluate the impact of photo catalytically treated water on seed germination, water samples obtained after degrading MO dye with the CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ catalyst were utilized for germinating pea seeds (Pisum sativum). Comparisons were made using untreated MO dye solution (100 ppm) and distilled water as controls. Seeds were washed, soaked for 24 h (considered day 0), and monitored for germination. The results, summarized in Table 3 a, reveal that germination commenced within 24–36 h in all treatments except for the untreated MO dye solution.

The CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ catalyst-treated water demonstrated the fastest germination rate, surpassing other degraded water treatments and controls (Fig. 9 ). In contrast, seeds exposed to untreated MO dye showed no seedling development after initial germination, likely due to the dye's toxicity or interference with nutrient uptake. Remarkably, seeds treated with CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ degraded water achieved a 100 % germination rate, outperforming treatments with MoO3:1 %La3+, MoO3, and the distilled water control. The root and shoot lengths of seeds treated with CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ catalyst-degraded water exhibited the most significant enhancement, as detailed in Table 3b. This improvement can be attributed to the biocompatible nature of the CDs derived from neem leaves, which interacted with seeds' biochemical processes, accelerating germination and promoting healthy growth (Fig. S8a). The Plant Inhibition Growth (PIG) value was highest for MO dye-treated water, highlighting its toxicity, while MoO3-treated water showed reduced PIG values. For CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ catalyst-degraded water, the PIG value was negligible, underscoring its non-toxic nature and the effectiveness of the CDs in degrading MO dye (Fig. S8b). Germination Ability was found to be 100 % for all degraded water treatments and controls, with zero germination for untreated MO dye water (Table 3a). Vigor Index I found to be highest for CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ catalyst-degraded water, outperforming the control (Fig. S8c). The combination of MoO3:La3+ and CDs provides synergistic benefits for seed germination. MoO3 NPs offer antioxidant properties and enhance enzyme activity, while CDs improve water absorption, nutrient delivery, and light utilization [88]. Together, these properties promote faster germination and healthier early seedling growth. The germination index, a key metric for evaluating seedling growth potential, was highest for CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ degraded water, further supporting its positive effect on seed development (Fig. S8d). This study highlights the dual functionality of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ as a photocatalyst and a promoter of sustainable agricultural applications. The photocatalytic efficiency of the synthesized catalyst not only enabled effective MO dye degradation but also facilitated enhanced seed germination and early seedling growth. These results demonstrate the potential of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ catalysts in environmental remediation and sustainable agriculture, offering a promising avenue for addressing wastewater toxicity and promoting plant productivity.

To evaluate the applicability of MoO3, MoO3:1 %La3+, and CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs for dactyloscopy, LFPs from the same donor were developed on aluminum foil. The LFPs were generated by brushing the synthesized powders smoothly onto the foil. Lipid residues in the LFP ridges interacted hydrophobically with the powders, enabling visualization of the FPs [89]. Fig. 10
(a, e, i) show the papillary ridge patterns developed using MoO3, MoO3:1 %La3+, and CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs, respectively. Among these, CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs adhered selectively and uniformly to the ridges, while MoO3 and MoO3:1 %La3+ powders spread across both ridges and furrows. This uniform adhesion resulted in better definition and clarity of friction ridge patterns for CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs. Grayscale pixel analysis confirmed a high contrast between ridges and grooves for CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs (Fig. 10j). In contrast, MoO3:1 %La3+ powders, while better than MoO3, exhibited lower resolution and less uniform grayscale values, leading to poor-quality FP images (Fig. 10b and f). The 3D interactive surface plots of the developed FPs, extracted using ImageJ software, are shown in Fig. 10(c, g, h). The surface plots for CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs revealed a more uniform distribution of the material, consistent with the experimental images. Grayscale pixel values retrieved along green lines further confirmed that CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs provided superior recognition efficiency compared to MoO3 and MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs. Consequently, CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs were selected for further studies focusing on fluorescence intensity, resolution, and adhesion [90]. The applicability of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs for LFP development was further tested on various substrates, including OHP sheets, compact discs (CDs), and wood. High-contrast, high-resolution LFPs were successfully developed on all tested surfaces, as shown in Fig. S9(a-c). The type of substrate influenced the quality of the developed FPs. On OHP sheets, LFPs exhibited exceptional sharpness and homogeneity, consistent with the pixel profile analysis (Fig. S9(a1)). While FPs on porous surfaces displayed some non-uniformity in grayscale values, ridges and furrows remained distinguishable. The 3D interactive surface plots over the delta region (Fig. S9(a2-c2)) revealed uniformNP distribution on both non-porous and semi-porous surfaces.

The CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs enhanced FP visibility, allowing clear differentiation of ridges and furrows. The uniform distribution of NPs further confirmed the high selectivity and efficacy of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs for LFP development. CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs demonstrated superior sensitivity and selectivity for LFP development compared to MoO3 and MoO3:1 %La3+ NPs. Their uniform adhesion, high-resolution imaging, and fluorescence properties make them an excellent material for dactyloscopy applications across various substrates. These findings highlight the potential of CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs in forensic science, particularly for developing clear and high-contrast FPs on diverse surfaces [91].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of YOLOv8x in LFP detection and analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The YOLOv8x model, a cutting-edge object detection algorithm, offers significant advancements in the detection and analysis of LFPs. This method leverages deep learning techniques to identify intricate FP patterns, including minutiae such as bifurcations, ridge endings, loops, and scars, making it invaluable in forensic and biometric applications. To enable YOLOv8x for FP analysis, the algorithm is trained on a dataset of annotated FP images, where each image includes bounding boxes marking key minutiae features. This training phase exposes the network to a diverse set of FPs and annotations, allowing the model to learn the distinctive patterns and features unique to FPs [92]. Once trained, the model processes unseen FP images by extracting features through its convolutional and pooling layers. It predicts the location and class of minutiae features, generating bounding boxes with associated class probabilities and confidence scores. This automated detection capability is highly effective for applications in access control, law enforcement, and biometric systems [93].

ce:list-item.ce:para: Pre-processing: The input FP image undergoes pre-processing to enhance contrast and reduce noise, preparing it for analysis [94]. The pre-processed image is then passed through the YOLOv8x network, which extracts features at multiple scales to capture both low-level details and complex patterns (Fig. 11 a).

Training Options: YOLOv8x can be trained either from scratch or with pretrained weights, depending on the specificity of the task. Pretrained weights expedite the training process by leveraging prior learning, while training from scratch is ideal for specialized datasets significantly different from standard datasets (Fig. 11b).

Post-Processing: Predictions from YOLOv8x are refined in a post-processing stage, where low-confidence detections are discarded, and bounding boxes are adjusted for accuracy. Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) ensures that overlapping boxes for the same feature are reduced, retaining only the most confident detection (Fig. 11c). The result is a well-organized output comprising detected minutiae features with their bounding boxes and class labels.

Performance Evaluation: The performance of YOLOv8x is assessed using standard metrics, including precision, recall, F1-score, Intersection over Union (IoU), and mean average precision (mAP). Precision: Measures the proportion of true positives among all predicted positives [95]:
(10)
Precision
=
TP
Tp
+
FP

ce:section.ce:para:
Recall: Measures the proportion of true positives among all actual positives [96]:
(11)
Recall
=
TP
Tp
+
FN

F1-Score: Balances precision and recall [97]:
(12)
Recall
=
2 xPxR
P + R

IoU: Evaluates the overlap between predicted and ground truth bounding boxes, where an IoU > 0.5 indicates a true positive. mAP: Aggregates the average precision across multiple classes, providing an overall measure of model accuracy [98]. This model, based on the YOLOv8x architecture, effectively detects and classifies FP patterns, demonstrating high precision and recall [99,100]. It identifies critical minutiae features, such as bifurcations, where a ridge splits, and ridge ends, where a ridge terminates. The confusion matrices show that the model achieves 91 % accuracy for bifurcations, with a 100 % true negative rate and no false negatives (Fig. S11b). For ridge ends, the model achieves 84 % accuracy, also with a 100 % true negative rate and no false negatives (Fig. S11g). Precision and recall improve steadily during training, reducing false positives and improving the model's ability to capture all relevant minutiae (Fig. S11c, S11d, S11h, S11i). The mAP50 and mAP50-95 values indicate enhanced detection and localization, with increasing values reflecting improved accuracy in feature detection and bounding box placement (Fig. S11e, S11j).

The YOLOv8x model is specifically trained to detect unique FP patterns such as loops and scars. A loop is a pattern where ridges enter from one side, curve around, and exit on the same side (Fig. S12a), while a scar indicates damage or alteration to the FP pattern (Fig. S12f). The model performs well in recognizing these features, achieving 84 % accuracy in identifying loops, with no false negatives and a 16 % false positive rate (Fig. S12b). For scars, the model achieves 99 % precision, accurately identifying the background 100 % of the time, but mistakenly classifying 1 % of background regions as scars (Fig. S1

2g). Precision and recall metrics show steady improvement over training, with precision reducing false positives and recall enhancing the model's ability to capture all relevant features (Fig. S12c, S12d, S12h, S12i). The mAP50 and mAP50-95 values indicate improvements in both detection and localization, with higher values reflecting better accuracy in identifying and precisely bounding minutiae (Fig. S12e, S12j). These metrics highlight the model's effectiveness in distinguishing between FP patterns and background noise, while also identifying areas for potential improvement [101].

The results for bifurcation and ridge end features after 90 epochs are presented in Fig. S13 (a-f & g-l), showing the performance improvement of the YOLOv8x model compared to the baseline. Precision, recall, and mAP curves (Fig. S13 and S14) indicate that the enhanced model achieves near-optimal performance, with values approaching 0.9, demonstrating superior detection results. After 90 epochs, the model's box loss, classification loss, and distribution focal loss are also shown, with improvements in detection confidence, fewer missed and incorrect detections, and more detailed feature detection (Fig. S13 a-b & g-h). The recall-confidence, precision-confidence, precision-recall, and F1-confidence curves further highlight the model's robust performance across different confidence levels, with optimal performance occurring at a confidence level of 1 (Fig. S13 c-f & i-l). The YOLOv8x model's detection of loop and scar features is depicted in Fig. S14, showing corresponding loss function curves and evaluation metrics. Visual results in Fig. S15 and S16 display labelled features, with bounding boxes indicating the precise locations of bifurcations, ridge ends, loops, and scars. Each feature is annotated with a confidence score, showcasing the model's accurate feature localization. The correlograms in Fig. S15 (c & d) and S16 (c & d) provide insights into the relationships and correlations between detected features, with colour intensity reflecting the strength of these correlations. These results confirm the model's robustness and practical value for applications like secure key generation, demonstrating its ability to generalize across varying image qualities and structures.

YOLOv8x demonstrated exceptional capability in high-resolution analysis, providing detailed visualizations of FP features, including bifurcations, ridge endings, loops, and scars. This level of accuracy is vital for various applications, such as secure key generation, forensic investigations, and identity verification, where precise identification of these minutiae is crucial for ensuring reliability. Additionally, the model showcased impressive cross-platform adaptability, performing effectively across a wide range of datasets and application domains. This versatility allows YOLOv8x to handle diverse FP patterns and substrates, making it suitable for different environments and use cases. Moreover, by detecting minutiae features with high precision and recall, YOLOv8x significantly enhances security applications, particularly in secure authentication systems. Its ability to accurately recognize FP features ensures the integrity and reliability of biometric systems, while also improving the quality and trustworthiness of forensic evidence, paving the way for its broader adoption in security and law enforcement applications [102]. The YOLOv8x model offers a powerful solution for LFP detection and analysis. Its robust performance in identifying minutiae features, combined with its scalability and adaptability, makes it an invaluable tool in forensic and biometric applications. By leveraging advanced metrics and visualization techniques, YOLOv8x ensures accurate and efficient FP recognition, paving the way for its integration into a wide range of security and law enforcement systems.

This study presents the development of an innovative photocatalyst CDs integrated with MoO3:1 %La3+ to form CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs designed for the efficient degradation of MO dye. The CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs exhibited exceptional sensing capabilities, achieving a low detection limit of 0.032 μM for MO dye within a concentration range of 0–50 μM. Their photocatalytic efficiency was outstanding, with 99.4 % degradation of MO dye within 70 min under solar irradiation. Recyclability tests showed that the NCs maintained both catalytic performance and structural integrity over multiple cycles. Additionally, an environmental safety assessment using a seed germination test with Pisum sativum confirmed complete detoxification of the treated water, allowing successful germination. Beyond environmental remediation, the CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs also exhibited remarkable potential in forensic applications. They enhanced the visualization of Level I–III LFP details, providing high clarity and resolution crucial for forensic analysis. Additionally, the integration of YOLOv8x, an advanced object detection algorithm, significantly improved FP recognition by enabling rapid and precise identification of minutiae points, ridge patterns, and other FP features. YOLOv8x demonstrated superior performance compared to traditional methods, offering enhanced accuracy and processing speed suitable for forensic investigations and biometric authentication systems. This research establishes CDs/MoO3:1 %La3+ NCs as a dual-purpose solution, addressing industrial dye pollution with environmental and agricultural benefits while simultaneously contributing to advancements in forensic FP analysis. The findings underscore the potential of this novel photocatalyst for sustainable applications across multiple domains.
